title: Tutorial for making form validation with javascript
summary: In this tutorial I am will make a form validation script with javascript which will notify the user that the form field is empty.
textbody:
At first we need to assign an id attribute in our form element. Then in our script we need to make a function


function formValidate(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
}

Parameter e is an event of the form element, then we are preventing the form to submit itself with e.preventDefault() method;

Then we need to get all our form element by assigning a variable with var elms = e.target.elements. And will iterate through all our form elements with for loop.


function formValidate(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    var elms = e.target.elements;
    for (var i = 0; i < elms.length; i++) {
        
    }
}

Now we need to make a logic statement that when the form element is empty we will create a div element which will have a text content "Form field can not be empty".

function formValidate(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    var elms = e.target.elements;
    for (var i = 0; i < elms.length; i++) {
        if (elms[i].value === "") {
            var error = document.createElement('div');
            error.textContent = "Form field can not be empty.";
        }
    }
}

After that we will assign a class name of that error div element with error.className = "validate-error"; and we will add this element in the parent element of that empty input field.

function formValidate(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    var elms = e.target.elements;
    for (var i = 0; i < elms.length; i++) {
        if (elms[i].value === "") {
            var error = document.createElement('div');
            error.className = "validate-error";
            error.textContent = "Form field can not be empty.";
            elms[i].parentElement.appendChild(error);
        }
    }
}

We are almost finished, now we will move the cursor in that corresponding empty field for the user. And we will output(return) a false Boolean value. It means that our function will stop here and will return a false value. All statement we put after the return statement will not work, I mean will not execute.

function formValidate(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    var elms = e.target.elements;
    for (var i = 0; i < elms.length; i++) {
        if (elms[i].value === "") {
            var error = document.createElement('div');
            error.className = "validate-error";
            error.textContent = "Form field can not be empty.";
            elms[i].parentElement.appendChild(error);
            elms[i].focus();
            return false;
        }
    }
}

After the if statement block, we will make the form to submit itself with e.target.submit();

And we will make another if logic statement before our first logic statement that will check that parent element of that empty input element has a div element with .validate-error class. If that parent element has that child element we will remove that child element.

function formValidate(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    var elms = e.target.elements;
    for (var i = 0; i < elms.length; i++) {
        if (elms[i].parentElement.querySelector('.validate-error')) {
            elms[i].parentElement.removeChild(elms[i].parentElement.querySelector('.validate-error'));
        }
        if (elms[i].value === "") {
            var error = document.createElement('div');
            error.className = "validate-error";
            error.textContent = "Form field can not be empty.";
            elms[i].parentElement.appendChild(error);
            elms[i].focus();
            return false;
        }
    }
    e.target.submit();
}

Our main form validation function has been made, now we need to call this function.

function init() {
    var signUPForm = document.getElementById('sign-up-form');
    if (signUPForm) {
        signUPForm.addEventListener('submit', formValidate);
    }
}
window.addEventListener('load', init);

Full code

function init() {
    var signUPForm = document.getElementById('sign-up-form');
    if (signUPForm) {
        signUPForm.addEventListener('submit', formValidate);
    }
}
window.addEventListener('load', init);
function formValidate(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    var elms = e.target.elements;
    for (var i = 0; i < elms.length; i++) {
        if (elms[i].parentElement.querySelector('.validate-error')) {
            elms[i].parentElement.removeChild(elms[i].parentElement.querySelector('.validate-error'));
        }
        if (elms[i].value === "") {
            var error = document.createElement('div');
            error.className = "validate-error";
            error.textContent = "Form field can not be empty.";
            elms[i].parentElement.appendChild(error);
            elms[i].focus();
            return false;
        }
    }
    e.target.submit();
}
author: Surajit Basak

title: Command Line Tool is very time efficient and takes less job
summary: Some people scared of Command line tool. But everyday they are giving their command to someone. Like if you need water you ask your
textbody:
Some people scared of Command line tool. But everyday they are giving their command to someone. Like if you need water you ask your mom/brother/sister for it, you are asking something and someone doing for you. Command line tool is something like that, you are giving command to your system and your system doing it for you. Command line tool is much faster than your visuall task. A simple example:

You want to create 3 file a) Index.html, b) style.css and c) script.js for your new project. If you are doing it visually what you will do is at first create a directory with right click then enter on that directory and again right click to it, then from the context menu you are selecting new >> file >> giving file name 3 times.

But on command line:

cd project/
mkdir my_project_01
cd my_project_01
touch index.html style.css script.css

or for efficient use, you might do:

mkdir project/my_project_01
cd project/my_project_01
touch index.html style.css script.css

So you can see command line tool is very time efficient and takes less job.
author: Surajit Basak

title: Sass is a time saver for web designing
summary: Sass is a time saver, awesome tools to compile CSS. I am loving this CSS preprocessor. Lots of useful things is going to happen, e.g. css variables
textbody:
Sass is a time saver, awesome tools to compile CSS. I am loving this CSS preprocessor. Lots of useful things is going to happen, e.g. css variables, nesting css declarations, mixins and lots of awesome stuff.

Just to remind, for using sass on your OS(Windows or Ubuntu or Linux based distros) there are lots of apps there. I am using compass to compile sass file to css file. On terminal I need to type:

cd project/directory/
compass init
compass watch (if you are in a project directory) or
compass watch project/directory/

Now compass will monitor on your sass file(.scss), whenever there is a change on your sass file it will compile your sass file into css file.

Some good example

Declaring variables

$font-serif: 'Lora', serif;
$font-sans: 'Roboto', 'Open Sans', sans-serif;

Nesting:

heading .banner {
    background-color: green;
    font-family: $font-sans;
    padding: 20px 0;
    .logo {
        margin: 0 20px 0 0;
        float: left;
        img {
            display: block;
            opacity: 0.95;
        }
    }
}

Will create on CSS file

heading .banner {
    background-color: green;
    font-family: 'Roboto', 'Open Sans', sans-serif;
    padding: 20px 0;
}

heading .banner .logo {
    margin: 0 20px 0 0;
    float: left;
}

heading .banner .logo img {
    display: block;
    opacity: 0.95;
}

MIxin (wow like a function)

@mixin title-style ($color: red, $size: 24px) {
    font-size: $size;
    font-family: $font-serif;
    margin: 10px 0;
    color: $color;
    font-weight: 200;
}

Now you know what? I can use it anywhere to style my element.

header h1 { @include title-style(green, 54px); }

section .content h2 { @include title-style(red, 48px); }

section .content h3 { @include title-style(); }


So, as on css file it will create:

header h1 {
    font-size: $size;
    font-family: 'Lora', serif;
    margin: 10px 0;
    color: green;
    font-weight: 200;
}

This is the reason I love mixin.
author: Surajit Basak

title: Making alias
summary: Making alias for CLI command is very useful, it saves my time and need to type less. Lets say I want to go localhost directory by typing below command
textbody:
Making alias for CLI command is very useful, it saves my time and need to type less. Lets say I want to go localhost directory by typing below command:

cd /var/www/html

I can see that it is tedius type and takes little time, so how it will be if you have shortcut method of it?

To do so, I need to type on my CLI

nano ~/.bashrc or gedit ~/.bashrc

It will open up a file in your editor, now for example do this:

alias localhost="cd /var/www/html/"

What it does is it holds this command in localhost variable(alias). On my terminal I need to type localhost and hit enter. It will let me to enter on your localhost folder.

Some good shortcuts made by me:

alias master='git checkout master'
alias ginit='git init';alias gad='git add .'
alias gcommit='git commit'
alias addremote='git remote origin'
alias gst='git status';alias glog='git log'
alias st='open -a "Sublime Text"'
alias server="python -m SimpleHTTPServer"
alias p="cd ~/projects"
alias localhost="cd /var/www/html/"
alias unityBottom="gsettings set com.canonical.Unity.Launcher launcher-position Bottom"
alias unityLeft="gsettings set com.canonical.Unity.Launcher launcher-position Left"
author: Surajit Basak

title: What is the best note taking application for personal use?
summary: Two days ago, when I was browsing on Ubuntu software center I saw an application named Zim 0.65 - A desktop wiki
textbody: Two days ago, when I was browsing on Ubuntu software center I saw an application named Zim 0.65 - A desktop wiki. I installed it, after opening that app I found it is so useful. It creates notes in text format when I write to it. But when I open that text file in Zim application it works like a charm. If I need to make 2nd level heading I just need to type ctrl+2, then voila! It creates 2nd level heading. It creates link automatically if it detects I am typing a link. I also found that if I type /home/Codes/Python and press a space bar it creates link in blue color. And when I click to it, it opens up that Python directory. It is awesome. Furthermore, if I type https://www.google.com and press spacebar it creates a link, and when I click to that link, it opens that link in my default browser. And if I write ~/Coders/info.txt and press a spacebar it creates a link, then if I click on that link it opens that file in my default text editor. Really a good note taking application for linux. It deserves five star rating.
author: Surajit Basak

title: How to make app symbolic link on Ubuntu like we create app shortcut on Windows?
summary: Helpful information, in future if I need to make a shortcut of any application it will help me to do that. Sometime
textbody:
Helpful information, in future if I need to make a shortcut of any application it will help me to do that. Sometimes, I need to use proprietary software and install it, it will help.

On terminal type 

sudo ln -s /app-source-directory/app-name /destination/app-name

┌── ln(1) link, ln -- make links
│ ┌── Create a symbolic link.
│ │ ┌── the path to the intended symlink
│ │ │ can use . or ~ or other relative paths
│ │ ┌─────┴────────┐
ln -s /path/to/original /path/to/symlink

 

└───────┬───────┘

└── the path to the original file/folder

can use . or ~ or other relative paths

$ echo content > original
$ ln -s original symlink
$ ls -la original symlink
-rw-r--r-- 1 grgarside staff 8 28 Jan 18:44 original
lrwxr-xr-x 1 grgarside staff 8 28 Jan 18:44 symlink -> original
$ cat symlink
author: Surajit Basak
